title: Putin can no longer shield ordinary Russians from the war he unleashed
gist: Ukraine's expanding deep-strike drone campaign in H1 2026—including hits on St. Petersburg during its economic forum and on oil refineries—is breaking down the Kremlin's wartime social contract that insulated major Russian cities from the war. The authors predict the Kremlin responds with more internet shutdowns, domestic-security spend, and escalation against Ukrainian cities, while ordinary Russians face fuel rationing and disrupted air travel.

themes.theme: Deep-strike campaign breaking the social contract

insights.claim: Ukraine's H1 2026 deep-strike drone campaign has materially escalated, hitting St. Petersburg during the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum and putting essentially anywhere in Russia within drone range, eroding the Kremlin's ability to shield core cities from the war.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Rising attacks on Russian core cities raise the war's domestic cost and the Russia-risk premium—watch for political/escalation spillover.
insights.locator: Ukraine’s recent drone strikes on St. Petersburg

insights.claim: Since 2022 the Kremlin's wartime social contract insulated Moscow and St. Petersburg from disruption in exchange for passive acceptance of the war, and the authors argue this model is now growing 'less reliable and more expensive to maintain.'
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: A weakening domestic political model raises the probability of harsher repression or escalation—both tail risks for the conflict's trajectory.
insights.quote: the political model that has protected Putin since 2022 is growing less reliable and more expensive to maintain.
insights.locator: None of this means the Putin regime is on the brink

insights.claim: Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian oil refineries are causing a mounting fuel crisis, with ordinary Russians facing restrictions on gasoline and diesel purchases in regions across the country.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Refinery strikes constraining Russian product supply support refined-product crack spreads and add an export-disruption watch item.
insights.quote: Ukrainian drone strikes on oil refineries are contributing to a mounting fuel crisis in regions across Russia.

insights.claim: The Kremlin has imposed sweeping new internet-access restrictions and mobile internet shutdowns, justified as anti-drone measures, even though Ukrainian attacks continue despite the crackdown.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Connectivity restrictions signal regime stress and weigh on Russian domestic digital/economic activity—a deteriorating-internals indicator.
insights.locator: The Kremlin has added to the darkening mood

insights.claim: The authors predict the Kremlin will respond with further internet shutdowns and more domestic-security spending, while escalating strikes against Ukrainian cities, civilian infrastructure, and transport to restore deterrence.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Points to intensified attacks on Ukrainian infrastructure—relevant to Ukrainian reconstruction/energy risk and escalation scenarios.
insights.locator: The most likely response from the Kremlin

insights.claim: Russia may accelerate hybrid operations in Europe to demonstrate it still controls the escalation ladder in the wider international arena.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Hybrid escalation into Europe is a contagion risk for European security and energy infrastructure—worth monitoring.
insights.locator: The Kremlin may also attempt to accelerate hybrid operations
